(February 13, 1915 – September 24, 2003)[[1]](#cite_note-1) was an American character actor who had roles in [Hollywood](/wiki/Hollywood_(film_industry) "Hollywood (film industry)") films and television from the 1950s onward, often portraying villains. One such role was the wrathfully jealous elephant handler Klaus from the [Oscar](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards")-winning film *[The Greatest Show on Earth](/wiki/The_Greatest_Show_on_Earth_(film) "The Greatest Show on Earth (film)")* (1952).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American actor (1915-2003)

| Lyle Bettger | |
| --- | --- |
| Bettger in *[The Greatest Show on Earth](/wiki/The_Greatest_Show_on_Earth_(film) "The Greatest Show on Earth (film)")* (1952) | |
| Born | (1915-02-13)February 13, 1915  [Philadelphia, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Philadelphia,_Pennsylvania "Philadelphia, Pennsylvania"), U.S. |
| Died | September 24, 2003(2003-09-24) (aged 88)  [Atascadero, California](/wiki/Atascadero,_California "Atascadero, California"), U.S. |
| Occupation | Actor |
| Years active | 1936–1980 |
| Spouse | Mary Gertrude Rolfe  ​  ​ (m. 1941; died 1996)​ |

**Lyle Stathem Bettger** (February 13, 1915 – September 24, 2003)[[1]](#cite_note-1) was an American character actor who had roles in [Hollywood](/wiki/Hollywood_(film_industry) "Hollywood (film industry)") films and television from the 1950s onward, often portraying villains. One such role was the wrathfully jealous elephant handler Klaus from the [Oscar](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards")-winning film *[The Greatest Show on Earth](/wiki/The_Greatest_Show_on_Earth_(film) "The Greatest Show on Earth (film)")* (1952).

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lyle_Bettger&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Bettger was born in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), the son of [Frank Bettger](/wiki/Frank_Bettger "Frank Bettger"), an infielder for the [St. Louis Cardinals](/wiki/St._Louis_Cardinals "St. Louis Cardinals") in 1910 (as Frank Betcher), successful insurance salesman and best-selling self-help author, and Mertie Stathem Bettger. He graduated from the [Haverford School](/wiki/Haverford_School "Haverford School") in [Haverford, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Haverford,_Pennsylvania "Haverford, Pennsylvania") and from the [American Academy of Dramatic Arts](/wiki/American_Academy_of_Dramatic_Arts "American Academy of Dramatic Arts") in New York City.[[2]](#cite_note-ecf-2)

## Stage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lyle_Bettger&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Stage")]

Bettger's theatrical debut was in *[Brother Rat](/wiki/Brother_Rat "Brother Rat")* at the *[Biltmore Theatre](/wiki/Samuel_J._Friedman_Theatre "Samuel J. Friedman Theatre")* in New York City in 1936. His [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre") credits include *Dance Night* (1938), *Summer Night* (1939), *The Flying Gerardos* (1940–1941), *The Moon Is Down* (1942), *All for All* (1943), *Oh, Brother!* (1945), *John Loves Mary* (1947–1948), and *Love Life* (1948–1949).[[3]](#cite_note-3)

When [Paramount](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures") sent a talent scout to see him, Bettger was signed to a three-year contract. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lyle_Bettger&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Film")]

Bettger's movie career began when he was cast in *The Lie* in 1949.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Movie columnist Frank Neill reported, "On the basis of his performance in the movie, he has been signed to a juicy contract."[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Later, Bettger was cast as the villain in the [film noir](/wiki/Film_noir "Film noir") *[No Man of Her Own](/wiki/No_Man_of_Her_Own_(1950_film) "No Man of Her Own (1950 film)")* (1950). He soon became a regular on the set of Westerns such as *[Denver and Rio Grande](/wiki/Denver_and_Rio_Grande_(film) "Denver and Rio Grande (film)")* (1952), *[The Great Sioux Uprising](/wiki/The_Great_Sioux_Uprising "The Great Sioux Uprising")* (1953), *[Forbidden](/wiki/Forbidden_(1953_film) "Forbidden (1953 film)")* (1953), *[Drums Across the River](/wiki/Drums_Across_the_River_(1954) "Drums Across the River (1954)")* (1954), *[Destry](/wiki/Destry_(1954) "Destry (1954)")* (1955), *[The Lone Ranger](/wiki/The_Lone_Ranger_(1956_film) "The Lone Ranger (1956 film)")*, (1956) and *[Gunfight at the O.K. Corral](/wiki/Gunfight_at_the_O.K._Corral_(film) "Gunfight at the O.K. Corral (film)")* (1957).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Bettger developed a reputation for playing the bad guy and excelled in villainous roles such as the menacing Joe Beacom in *[Union Station](/wiki/Union_Station_(film) "Union Station (film)")* (1950) and the cold-blooded [Nazi](/wiki/Nazi "Nazi") [Chief Officer](/wiki/Chief_Officer "Chief Officer") Kirchner in *[The Sea Chase](/wiki/The_Sea_Chase "The Sea Chase")* (1955). One of his later roles was in the 1969 film *[Impasse](/wiki/Impasse_(1969_film) "Impasse (1969 film)")* as a bigoted World War II veteran.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Radio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lyle_Bettger&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Radio")]

Programs on which Bettger appeared in [old-time radio](/wiki/Old-time_radio "Old-time radio") included *[Grand Central Station](/wiki/Grand_Central_Station_(radio) "Grand Central Station (radio)")*.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lyle_Bettger&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Television")]

Bettger made many appearances in dramatic roles on television, starring in the 1957 series *[The Court of Last Resort](/wiki/The_Court_of_Last_Resort "The Court of Last Resort")*[[7]](#cite_note-brooks-7) as well as guest starring on *[Hawaii Five-O](/wiki/Hawaii_Five-O_(1968_TV_series) "Hawaii Five-O (1968 TV series)")*, *[Rawhide](/wiki/Rawhide_(TV_series) "Rawhide (TV series)")*, *[The Tall Man](/wiki/The_Tall_Man_(TV_series) "The Tall Man (TV series)")*, *[The Rifleman](/wiki/The_Rifleman "The Rifleman")*, *[Gunsmoke](/wiki/Gunsmoke "Gunsmoke")* (as Polk, a murdering robber in 1962's S9E22 - "The Kite"), *[Bonanza](/wiki/Bonanza "Bonanza")*, *[Combat!](/wiki/Combat! "Combat!")*, *[Blue Light](/wiki/Blue_Light_(TV_series) "Blue Light (TV series)")*, *[The Time Tunnel](/wiki/The_Time_Tunnel "The Time Tunnel")*, *[Death Valley Days](/wiki/Death_Valley_Days "Death Valley Days")*, *[Laramie](/wiki/Laramie_(TV_series) "Laramie (TV series)")*, and *[Tales of Wells Fargo](/wiki/Tales_of_Wells_Fargo "Tales of Wells Fargo")* (as John Wesley Hardin). He is cast on the right side of the law as U.S. Marshall John Welker and hunts down the bank robber that killed his wife in the 1959 episode, "The Law and the Gun" of *[Zane Grey Theater](/wiki/Zane_Grey_Theater "Zane Grey Theater")*. This episode also features Michael Ansara, Paul Carr and Don Diamond(as Henry Seidel, the wife killer)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lyle_Bettger&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Bettger was married to Mary Gertrude Rolfe from 1941 until her death in 1996. They had three children: Lyle Jr., Frank, and Paula. She was an actress who played Henry Aldrich's sister in *[The Aldrich Family](/wiki/The_Aldrich_Family "The Aldrich Family")* on radio.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Bettger and his family moved to Hawaii in the late 1960s.[[9]](#cite_note-9)
